solutions.name: EtherCAT Servo Drive Solution
solutions.description: Complete EtherCAT servo drive solution combining FCE1100/FCE1353 EtherCAT slave controller with FCP32C335 DSP for high-performance motion control. This solution enables precise multi-axis synchronization with cycle times down to 125μs.

keyComponents.description: EtherCAT slave controller for communication interface
DSP for motor control algorithms and motion profiles
Industrial Ethernet PHY for reliable network connection
Isolated gate driver for power stage control
Hall-effect current sensors for motor phase feedback

coreAdvantages.title: High-Speed Real-Time Communication
coreAdvantages.description: 125μs EtherCAT cycle time with distributed clock synchronization enables precise multi-axis coordination for demanding motion control applications.

coreAdvantages.title: Precision Motor Control
coreAdvantages.description: 150ps HRPWM resolution combined with FOC algorithms delivers smooth, accurate motor control with minimal torque ripple.

coreAdvantages.title: Pin-to-Pin Compatibility
coreAdvantages.description: Drop-in replacement for ET1100 and TMS320F28335-based designs reduces development risk and accelerates time-to-market.

coreAdvantages.title: Comprehensive Protection
coreAdvantages.description: Integrated hardware and software protection features ensure safe operation and long system lifetime.

coreAdvantages.title: Comprehensive Development Support
coreAdvantages.description: Complete reference designs, software libraries, and technical documentation accelerate product development and reduce time-to-market.

customerCases.challenge: The customer needed a cost-effective servo drive solution for their new CNC machining center line. Their existing design used imported chips facing supply constraints and high costs. They required 125μs cycle time for precise contouring and multi-axis synchronization for complex 5-axis machining operations.
customerCases.solution: We implemented a servo drive solution using FCE1100 for EtherCAT communication and FCP32C335 for motor control. The pin-compatible design allowed direct replacement of existing components without PCB redesign. Custom firmware was developed to support CiA 402 drive profile with position, velocity, and torque control modes. The 150ps HRPWM enabled smooth motion for high-speed machining.
customerCases.result: The customer achieved 40% cost reduction compared to imported solutions while maintaining equivalent performance. The 125μs cycle time enabled precise contouring with surface finish quality matching their previous design. Supply security improved with domestic sourcing. The solution passed rigorous EMC testing and has been deployed in over 500 machines with excellent field reliability.

customerCases.challenge: A packaging machinery manufacturer needed to upgrade their servo system for higher speed and precision. Their existing system used analog drives with limited synchronization capability, resulting in poor coordination between multiple servo axes. They needed a digital solution with real-time synchronization for their 8-axis cartoning machine.
customerCases.solution: We designed a complete EtherCAT servo drive system using FCE1353 with integrated PHYs for reduced BOM complexity. The FCP32C335 DSP handled FOC control for all servo motors with synchronized motion profiles. Distributed clock synchronization ensured precise axis coordination. The solution included electronic camming and flying shear algorithms for high-speed packaging operations.
customerCases.result: Machine speed increased by 35% compared to the previous analog system. Product changeover time reduced from 30 minutes to 5 minutes through electronic camming. Synchronization accuracy improved to ±1 encoder count across all 8 axes. The customer reported significant reduction in mechanical wear due to smoother motion profiles. The solution has been replicated across their entire product line.

faeInsights.insight: The combination of FCE1100/FCE1353 with FCP32C335 creates a powerful servo drive platform that competes with established international solutions. In my experience supporting numerous servo drive projects, the key success factor is proper implementation of distributed clock synchronization. Many developers underestimate the importance of clock jitter and propagation delay compensation. The FCE1100's hardware DC support makes achieving <100ns synchronization accuracy straightforward when configured correctly. For motor control, the FCP32C335's HRPWM is a game-changer for high-performance applications. I've seen torque ripple reduced by 60% compared to standard PWM implementations. The 150ps resolution enables advanced modulation techniques that were previously only possible with FPGA-based solutions. One common pitfall is inadequate current sensing design - the shunt resistor value and amplifier bandwidth must be carefully matched to the switching frequency. I recommend starting with Funcience's reference designs and making incremental modifications rather than designing from scratch.
faeInsights.logic: Servo drive design follows a systematic approach: First, define your performance requirements - cycle time, synchronization accuracy, and control bandwidth. For most industrial applications, 250μs cycle time is sufficient, but high-speed packaging or CNC may need 125μs. Second, select the appropriate EtherCAT controller - FCE1100 for cost-sensitive 2-port designs or FCE1353 for integrated PHY applications. Third, design the current sensing and power stage with adequate bandwidth for your switching frequency. Fourth, implement the control algorithm starting with current loop, then velocity, then position. Fifth, tune the distributed clock parameters for synchronization. Finally, validate with dynamic performance testing under load conditions.
keyTakeaways: Start with Funcience reference designs for proven circuit implementations
Use FCE1353 for new designs to reduce BOM cost with integrated PHYs
Implement proper DC configuration for accurate multi-axis synchronization
Leverage HRPWM for high-performance applications requiring smooth motion
Validate current loop bandwidth before closing outer control loops
commonPitfalls: Inadequate power supply decoupling causing communication errors
Incorrect DC configuration leading to synchronization issues
Insufficient current sensing bandwidth causing control instability
Poor PCB layout introducing noise in analog signals
bestPractices: Use separate ground planes for digital and analog sections
Implement proper ESD protection on all external interfaces
Include test points for key signals during development
Plan for thermal management in the mechanical design
faeInsights.insightLogic: Servo drive design follows a systematic approach: First, define your performance requirements - cycle time, synchronization accuracy, and control bandwidth. For most industrial applications, 250μs cycle time is sufficient, but high-speed packaging or CNC may need 125μs. Second, select the appropriate EtherCAT controller - FCE1100 for cost-sensitive 2-port designs or FCE1353 for integrated PHY applications. Third, design the current sensing and power stage with adequate bandwidth for your switching frequency. Fourth, implement the control algorithm starting with current loop, then velocity, then position. Fifth, tune the distributed clock parameters for synchronization. Finally, validate with dynamic performance testing under load conditions.

In my experience supporting numerous servo drive projects, the key success factor is proper implementation of distributed clock synchronization. Many developers underestimate the importance of clock jitter and propagation delay compensation. The FCE1100's hardware DC support makes achieving <100ns synchronization accuracy straightforward when configured correctly. For motor control, the FCP32C335's HRPWM is a game-changer for high-performance applications. I've seen torque ripple reduced by 60% compared to standard PWM implementations. The 150ps resolution enables advanced modulation techniques that were previously only possible with FPGA-based solutions. One common pitfall is inadequate current sensing design - the shunt resistor value and amplifier bandwidth must be carefully matched to the switching frequency. I recommend starting with Funcience's reference designs and making incremental modifications rather than designing from scratch.

faqs.question: What is the minimum achievable EtherCAT cycle time with this solution?
faqs.answer: The FCE1100/FCE1353 supports cycle times down to 125μs, which is the minimum recommended for most servo drive applications. This cycle time enables control loop bandwidths up to 2kHz for the current loop. For less demanding applications, cycle times of 250μs or 1ms can be used to reduce processor loading. The actual achievable performance depends on the host DSP's processing capability and the complexity of control algorithms. The FCP32C335 can comfortably handle FOC calculations within 125μs cycle time for standard servo applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 125μs cycle time for high-performance applications. Longer cycle times can be used for lower bandwidth requirements.

faqs.question: How many axes can be synchronized using distributed clocks?
faqs.answer: The distributed clock feature can synchronize virtually unlimited axes in theory, limited only by network topology and cycle time. Practical implementations typically synchronize 8-32 axes on a single EtherCAT network. The synchronization accuracy is maintained regardless of the number of axes because each slave compensates for its own propagation delay. For very large systems with hundreds of axes, network segmentation may be used. The FCE1100/FCE1353 hardware DC implementation ensures <100ns jitter across all synchronized axes.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design your network topology based on application requirements. Contact us for multi-axis system design guidance.

faqs.question: What motor types are supported by the FOC implementation?
faqs.answer: The FCP32C335-based FOC implementation supports various motor types: Surface-mount PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor), Interior PMSM (IPMSM) with reluctance torque utilization, AC induction motors with rotor flux orientation, BLDC motors with sinusoidal commutation. The control algorithm automatically handles different motor parameters through configuration. Sensor-based operation uses quadrature encoders or resolvers. Sensorless operation is supported for applications where encoder mounting is not possible, though with reduced performance at very low speeds.
faqs.decisionGuide: Specify your motor type and feedback requirements. We provide motor parameter configuration assistance.

faqs.question: What is the advantage of HRPWM in servo applications?
faqs.answer: HRPWM (High-Resolution PWM) provides 150ps resolution compared to approximately 10ns for standard PWM at typical switching frequencies. This 60x improvement enables: Significantly reduced torque ripple, especially at low speeds where standard PWM quantization is most noticeable. Smoother motion profiles with finer position resolution. Better current control precision leading to improved dynamic response. Reduced audible noise from the motor. Ability to implement advanced modulation techniques. For high-performance servo applications, HRPWM is essential for achieving premium motion quality.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use HRPWM for high-performance servo drives. Standard PWM may be sufficient for general VFD applications.

faqs.question: How do I migrate from an existing ET1100 + TMS320F28335 design?
faqs.answer: Migration is straightforward due to pin-to-pin compatibility: Hardware migration involves direct component replacement - FCE1100 replaces ET1100, FCP32C335 replaces TMS320F28335. No PCB changes are required if the original design followed standard practices. Software migration requires updating header files and peripheral drivers to match Funcience register definitions. The control algorithms and application code remain largely unchanged. ESI file needs to be updated with FCE1100 identity information. Funcience provides migration guides, code examples, and technical support. Most customers complete migration within 2-4 weeks.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for migration support, reference code, and compatibility documentation.

faqs.question: What protection features are included in the solution?
faqs.answer: The solution includes comprehensive protection: Hardware protections implemented in gate drivers - overcurrent shutdown, undervoltage lockout, overtemperature protection. Software protections in DSP - overcurrent limiting, overvoltage protection, encoder loss detection, following error monitoring. Communication watchdogs - loss of EtherCAT communication triggers safe state. Safe torque off (STO) support for safety applications. All protections are coordinated to ensure safe shutdown and prevent equipment damage. Protection parameters are configurable for different application requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Protection features can be configured for your application. Contact us for protection scheme design assistance.

solutions.title: EtherCAT Servo Drive Solution
solutions.longDescription:
The EtherCAT Servo Drive Solution from funcience represents a comprehensive approach to addressing modern challenges in Industrial. This solution integrates advanced semiconductor technology with robust design methodologies to deliver exceptional performance and reliability.

At the core of this solution is a carefully selected portfolio of funcience components optimized for the target application. The design leverages the latest advances in power management, signal processing, and thermal management to achieve industry-leading efficiency and performance metrics.

Key technical features include high-efficiency power conversion, precise control algorithms, and comprehensive protection mechanisms. The solution supports wide operating ranges and maintains performance across varying environmental conditions. Advanced monitoring and diagnostic capabilities enable predictive maintenance and optimize system uptime.

Implementation is streamlined through comprehensive design support including reference designs, simulation models, and detailed application notes. BeiLuo's FAE team provides expert guidance throughout the development cycle from concept through production. Customization options are available to address specific application requirements.

The solution has been validated through extensive testing including functional verification, environmental stress screening, and long-term reliability assessment. Field deployment data demonstrates consistent performance and high customer satisfaction across diverse applications and operating conditions.
benefits: Reduced development time with proven reference design
Lower BOM cost through optimized component selection
Reliable performance validated in industrial environments
Flexible architecture adaptable to various applications

solutions.name: Industrial Ethernet I/O Solution
solutions.description: Flexible industrial Ethernet I/O solution using FCE1353 with integrated PHYs for reduced BOM and simplified design. Supports multiple industrial Ethernet protocols including EtherCAT, PROFINET, and Ethernet/IP through firmware configuration.

keyComponents.description: 3-port EtherCAT controller with dual integrated PHYs
Cost-effective DSP for I/O processing and protocol handling
Analog input multiplexer for multiple AI channels
Overvoltage and surge protection for field I/O

coreAdvantages.title: Reduced BOM Cost
coreAdvantages.description: FCE1353's integrated PHYs eliminate external Ethernet PHY chips, crystals, and associated components, reducing BOM cost by 30-40%.

coreAdvantages.title: Flexible Network Topology
coreAdvantages.description: 3-port design enables T-junction and branch topologies impossible with 2-port solutions, simplifying wiring in complex machines.

coreAdvantages.title: Multi-Protocol Support
coreAdvantages.description: Single hardware platform supports EtherCAT, PROFINET, and Ethernet/IP through firmware configuration, reducing SKU complexity.

coreAdvantages.title: High-Density I/O
coreAdvantages.description: Compact design supports up to 32 digital and 16 analog I/O channels in a small form factor module.

coreAdvantages.title: Comprehensive Development Support
coreAdvantages.description: Complete reference designs, software libraries, and technical documentation accelerate product development and reduce time-to-market.

customerCases.application: Modular I/O System for Flexible Manufacturing
customerCases.challenge: The customer needed a modular I/O system that could adapt to different machine configurations without hardware changes. Their existing system used fixed I/O modules, requiring large inventory and complex configuration management. They wanted a single hardware platform supporting multiple fieldbus protocols.
customerCases.solution: We developed a universal I/O module using FCE1353 for communication and FCP32C334 for I/O processing. The 3-port EtherCAT controller enabled flexible daisy-chain wiring with branch capability. Firmware configuration allowed the same hardware to operate as EtherCAT, PROFINET, or Ethernet/IP slave. Web-based configuration simplified setup without specialized tools.
customerCases.result: Inventory reduced by 60% through universal module design. Installation time decreased by 40% with flexible topology support. The customer standardized on one I/O platform across their entire product line. Field service calls reduced due to simplified configuration. The solution has been deployed in over 2000 installations with excellent reliability.

customerCases.challenge: A chemical plant needed remote I/O modules for a hazardous area installation. The modules needed to support both analog and digital I/O with high isolation ratings. Harsh environmental conditions included temperature extremes and chemical exposure. The customer required redundant network paths for critical measurements.
customerCases.solution: We designed ruggedized I/O modules using FCE1353 with its 3-port capability enabling ring network topology for redundancy. The design included 2500Vrms isolation, conformal coating for chemical resistance, and -40°C to +85°C temperature rating. 16-bit analog I/O provided precision measurement for process control. Dual network paths ensured communication reliability.
customerCases.result: Zero communication failures in 18 months of continuous operation. The redundant ring topology provided seamless failover during cable maintenance. Precision analog I/O improved process control accuracy by 25%. The ruggedized design eliminated field failures previously experienced with commercial-grade modules. The solution is now the standard for the customer's hazardous area installations.

faeInsights.insight: The FCE1353 is a game-changer for industrial I/O module design. The integrated PHYs dramatically simplify the design - I've seen customers reduce their PCB layer count from 6 to 4 layers and eliminate about 20 components. The 3-port capability is particularly valuable for machine builders who want flexible wiring options. I recently worked on a packaging machine where the third port allowed a clean T-junction topology that saved significant cable length compared to a pure daisy-chain. For multi-protocol support, the key is designing the hardware for the most demanding protocol (typically EtherCAT) and then using firmware to adapt to others. One important consideration is analog I/O design - the high-speed digital EtherCAT signals can couple noise into sensitive analog circuits. I always recommend careful PCB layout with separate ground planes and proper isolation. The FCP32C334 provides sufficient processing power for most I/O applications while keeping costs down. For high-density analog I/O, consider using external ADCs with SPI interface to the DSP.
faeInsights.logic: Industrial I/O design follows these principles: First, define your I/O requirements - channel count, types, and update rates. Second, select the communication protocol based on your customer's control system. Third, choose FCE1353 for new designs to leverage integrated PHYs and 3-port flexibility. Fourth, design the I/O circuits with appropriate isolation and protection for your environment. Fifth, implement protocol stack on the DSP with proper timing. Sixth, validate EMC performance with representative I/O loading. For multi-protocol designs, start with EtherCAT implementation as it's most demanding, then adapt for other protocols.
keyTakeaways: Use FCE1353 integrated PHYs to reduce BOM cost and PCB complexity
Leverage 3-port capability for flexible machine wiring topologies
Design hardware for EtherCAT, adapt firmware for other protocols
Implement proper isolation between digital and analog sections
Test EMC performance with all I/O channels active
commonPitfalls: Inadequate isolation causing ground loop issues
Insufficient protection on field I/O leading to damage
Poor analog layout resulting in noisy measurements
Inadequate power supply design for peak I/O loads
bestPractices: Use isolated DC-DC converters for field-side power
Implement TVS protection on all external connections
Separate analog and digital ground planes with single point connection
faeInsights.insightLogic: Industrial I/O design follows these principles: First, define your I/O requirements - channel count, types, and update rates. Second, select the communication protocol based on your customer's control system. Third, choose FCE1353 for new designs to leverage integrated PHYs and 3-port flexibility. Fourth, design the I/O circuits with appropriate isolation and protection for your environment. Fifth, implement protocol stack on the DSP with proper timing. Sixth, validate EMC performance with representative I/O loading. For multi-protocol designs, start with EtherCAT implementation as it's most demanding, then adapt for other protocols.
faeInsights.content: The FCE1353 is a game-changer for industrial I/O module design. The integrated PHYs dramatically simplify the design - I've seen customers reduce their PCB layer count from 6 to 4 layers and eliminate about 20 components. The 3-port capability is particularly valuable for machine builders who want flexible wiring options. I recently worked on a packaging machine where the third port allowed a clean T-junction topology that saved significant cable length compared to a pure daisy-chain. For multi-protocol support, the key is designing the hardware for the most demanding protocol (typically EtherCAT) and then using firmware to adapt to others. One important consideration is analog I/O design - the high-speed digital EtherCAT signals can couple noise into sensitive analog circuits. I always recommend careful PCB layout with separate ground planes and proper isolation. The FCP32C334 provides sufficient processing power for most I/O applications while keeping costs down. For high-density analog I/O, consider using external ADCs with SPI interface to the DSP.

faqs.question: What is the advantage of 3-port EtherCAT for I/O modules?
faqs.answer: The third port enables flexible network topologies that are impossible with 2-port controllers. You can create T-junctions where one I/O module branches to two separate lines, useful for modular machine designs. Ring topologies for redundancy are possible, ensuring communication continues if one cable fails. Star configurations from a central point reduce cable runs in some applications. The third port can also be used for diagnostic access without interrupting the main network. For machine builders, this flexibility simplifies wiring and reduces cable costs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use FCE1353 3-port capability for flexible wiring or redundancy requirements. FCE1100 is sufficient for simple daisy-chain topologies.

faqs.question: How does multi-protocol support work on the same hardware?
faqs.answer: Multi-protocol support is implemented through firmware configuration: The hardware is designed to meet the most demanding protocol requirements (typically EtherCAT). Different firmware images implement the specific protocol stack - EtherCAT slave stack, PROFINET IO device, or Ethernet/IP adapter. Protocol selection is made at startup through configuration pins or EEPROM settings. The same physical Ethernet interface handles all protocols. Application layer (I/O processing) remains consistent across protocols. Customers can stock one hardware SKU and configure for the specific protocol as needed. Funcience provides reference firmware for each supported protocol.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design for EtherCAT hardware requirements, use firmware for protocol selection. Contact us for multi-protocol firmware packages.

faqs.question: What isolation is required for industrial I/O?
faqs.answer: Industrial I/O typically requires multiple levels of isolation: Channel-to-bus isolation of 2500Vrms minimum for safety and noise immunity. Channel-to-channel isolation prevents ground loops between different field devices. Power isolation separates field-side power from system power. Digital isolators (magnetic or capacitive) provide high-speed isolation for digital signals. Isolation amplifiers or ADCs with isolated interfaces for analog signals. The specific isolation requirements depend on your application standards and environment. Funcience reference designs demonstrate proven isolation implementations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Match isolation specifications to your application requirements and industry standards. Contact us for isolation design guidance.

faqs.question: How many I/O points can be handled per module?
faqs.answer: I/O capacity depends on the specific implementation: Digital I/O - up to 32 inputs and 32 outputs per module using port expanders or FPGA. Analog I/O - typically 8-16 channels limited by ADC/DAC channel count and processing time. Mixed I/O - common configurations include 16DI/16DO/8AI/4AO. The FCP32C334 can process 32 I/O points with 100μs update time. For higher densities, consider using external I/O expanders or FPGA. Practical limits are often determined by connector size and thermal considerations rather than processing capacity.
faqs.decisionGuide: Define your I/O requirements and we can recommend optimal channel count and configuration.

faqs.question: What analog I/O resolutions are supported?
faqs.answer: The solution supports various analog I/O resolutions: 16-bit resolution is standard for industrial applications, providing 65536 counts for precise measurement. 12-bit resolution can be used for less critical applications to reduce cost. Input ranges include 0-10V, ±10V, 0-20mA, and 4-20mA standard industrial signals. Output ranges match input ranges for consistency. Overrange capability allows measurement beyond nominal range (e.g., -10% to +110%). The FCP32C334's 12-bit ADC can be supplemented with external 16-bit ADCs for higher resolution requirements. Funcience reference designs include both options.
faqs.decisionGuide: 16-bit resolution is recommended for most applications. Contact us for analog I/O design options.

faqs.question: How is the web-based configuration implemented?
faqs.answer: Web-based configuration provides easy setup without specialized tools: The FCP32C334 runs a lightweight web server accessible via the Ethernet port. Users connect with a standard web browser to access configuration pages. Configuration includes I/O type assignment, scaling factors, filter settings, and network parameters. Changes can be saved to EEPROM for persistent storage. Diagnostic pages show real-time I/O values and communication status. Firmware updates can be performed through the web interface. The web interface is password protected for security. Funcience provides reference web interface code that can be customized.
faqs.decisionGuide: Web configuration simplifies field setup. Contact us for web interface implementation support.

solutions.title: Industrial Ethernet I/O Solution
solutions.subtitle: Complete EtherCAT I/O module solution with integrated communication and processing
solutions.description: Comprehensive industrial I/O solution using Funcience FCE1353 EtherCAT controller with integrated PHYs for distributed control systems and modular I/O applications.
solutions.longDescription:
The Industrial Ethernet I/O Solution from Funcience provides a complete platform for building distributed I/O modules using EtherCAT communication. Based on the FCE1353 3-port EtherCAT slave controller with integrated PHYs, this solution enables compact, cost-effective I/O modules for factory automation.

The solution supports digital I/O, analog I/O, and specialized interfaces like temperature sensors and encoder inputs. The 3-port design allows flexible network topology with daisy-chain and branch configurations. Integrated PHYs eliminate external Ethernet components, reducing BOM cost and PCB complexity.

coreAdvantages.title: Integrated PHYs
coreAdvantages.description: Dual integrated PHYs reduce BOM cost and complexity

coreAdvantages.title: 3-Port Design
coreAdvantages.description: Flexible network topology with daisy-chain and branch

coreAdvantages.title: Fast Cycle Time
coreAdvantages.description: 125μs minimum cycle time for real-time control

coreAdvantages.title: Complete Reference
coreAdvantages.description: Full design package with schematics and firmware

customerCases.challenge: Needed compact, cost-effective EtherCAT I/O modules with flexible network topology.
customerCases.solution: Implemented FCE1353-based I/O modules with 3-port design and integrated PHYs.
customerCases.result: Successfully deployed in production with excellent field reliability.

faeInsights.insight: The FCE1353's integrated PHYs and 3-port design make it ideal for I/O module applications. The third port enables T-junction topologies that simplify wiring in complex machines.
faeInsights.logic: Solution architecture: FCE1353 handles EtherCAT communication → FCP32C335 processes I/O signals → Modular design enables various I/O configurations.
keyTakeaways: Integrated PHYs significantly reduce BOM cost
commonPitfalls: Poor PCB layout affecting signal integrity
Missing ESD protection on I/O lines
bestPractices: Follow reference design for magnetics and termination
Use Kelvin connections for critical signals
Include ESD protection on all external interfaces

faqs.question: What I/O configurations are supported?
faqs.answer: The Industrial Ethernet I/O Solution supports various I/O configurations including digital inputs/outputs (24V industrial standard), analog inputs (0-10V, 4-20mA), analog outputs, temperature sensor inputs (RTD, thermocouple), and encoder interfaces. The FCP32C335 DSP provides sufficient processing power and peripheral interfaces to implement diverse I/O functions. Reference designs are available for common I/O module types.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select I/O configuration based on your application needs. Contact us for custom I/O module design support.

solutions.name: Industrial Ethernet I/O Solution

seoTitle: Funcience Solutions | EtherCAT & DSP Applications | LiTong Electronics
seoDescription: Explore Funcience application solutions including EtherCAT servo drives, industrial I/O systems, and motor control implementations. Complete reference designs available.

faqs.question: What application solutions does Funcience provide?
faqs.answer: Funcience provides comprehensive application solutions for industrial automation including EtherCAT servo drive solutions combining FCE1100/FCE1353 with FCP32C335 DSP, and industrial Ethernet I/O solutions using FCE1353 with integrated PHYs. These solutions include complete reference designs, BOM lists, and implementation guidance for rapid product development.
faqs.decisionGuide: Browse our solutions below to find the right application for your needs. Contact our FAE team for detailed implementation support.

faqs.question: How can I implement a Funcience solution in my product?
faqs.answer: Implementing a Funcience solution involves several steps: First, select the appropriate solution based on your application requirements. Review the reference design and technical specifications. Use the provided BOM to source components. Start with the evaluation kit to validate the solution for your application. Adapt the reference design to your specific requirements. Funcience provides technical support throughout the implementation process including schematic review and debugging assistance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with an evaluation kit to validate the solution. Contact LiTong for implementation support and design review services.

faqs.question: What support is available for solution implementation?
faqs.answer: Funcience and LiTong provide comprehensive support for solution implementation including: Detailed reference designs with schematics and PCB layouts. Complete BOM lists with component recommendations. Software libraries and sample code. Evaluation kits for rapid prototyping. Technical documentation and application notes. FAE support for design review and troubleshooting. Training sessions on solution implementation. This support ecosystem ensures successful product development with minimal risk.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact LiTong for solution implementation support. We provide design review, debugging assistance, and training resources.

faqs.question: Can Funcience solutions be customized for specific applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, Funcience solutions can be customized to meet specific application requirements. The reference designs serve as starting points that can be modified for different power levels, I/O configurations, or environmental requirements. Funcience's FAE team can provide guidance on customization including component selection, circuit modifications, and software adaptations. For high-volume applications, Funcience can also provide customized firmware or additional features. Contact us to discuss your specific customization needs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us to discuss customization requirements for your specific application. We can provide tailored solutions.

faqs.question: What is included in a Funcience reference design?
faqs.answer: Funcience reference designs include comprehensive documentation and resources: Complete schematic diagrams with component specifications. PCB layout files and design guidelines. Bill of Materials (BOM) with recommended suppliers. Software libraries and sample code. Technical documentation including user manuals and application notes. Evaluation kits for hardware validation. The reference designs demonstrate best practices and can be adapted for production use with appropriate review and qualification.
faqs.decisionGuide: Reference designs provide complete implementation guidance. Contact us for access to design files and documentation.
